note_data.title: Kinematics
keyPoints: One radian is defined as the angle in the sector of a circle when the arc length equals the radius of the circle
A complete circle contains 2π radians, allowing conversion between degrees and radians using π/180
Angular speed (ω) = v/r = 2π/T = 2πf, where v is linear speed, r is radius, T is time period, and f is frequency
Objects in circular motion have constantly changing velocity due to changing direction, requiring centripetal acceleration
Centripetal acceleration a = v²/r = ω²r, always directed toward the center of the circle
Centripetal force F = mv²/r = mω²r, acts perpendicular to motion toward the circle's center
Gravitational fields are areas where objects with mass experience non-contact forces
Newton's law of gravitation: F = Gm₁m₂/r², where force is proportional to mass product and inversely proportional to distance squared
Gravitational field strength g = F/m in uniform fields, and g = GM/r² in radial fields

definitions.definition: The angle in the sector of a circle when the arc length of that sector is equal to the radius of the circle
The angle turned through by an object in any given direction in radians
The angle an object moves through per unit time
The acceleration of an object moving in a circular path, always directed toward the center of the circle
The force that acts perpendicular to the motion of an object moving in a circle, always toward the center
The force per unit mass exerted by a gravitational field on an object

howToAnswer.instruction: Give one fact in one sentence. No explanation needed.
Give the point + reason using 'because' or 'this is because'
Show working clearly with correct formula, substitution and final answer with units
Draw clear axes with labels, correct shape and key values marked

modelAnswer.question: A cricket ball is hit from ground level at Gaddafi Stadium with an initial velocity of 25 m s⁻¹ at an angle of 30° to the horizontal. Calculate the maximum height reached by the ball. [g = 9.8 m s⁻²]
modelAnswer.answer:
Initial vertical velocity component: uy = 25 sin 30° = 12.5 m s⁻¹
At maximum height, final vertical velocity vy = 0
Using v² = u² + 2as: 0² = (12.5)² + 2(-9.8)s
Maximum height s = 156.25/(2 × 9.8) = 8.0 m
markBreakdown: Mark 1: Correct identification of vertical component uy = 12.5 m s⁻¹
Mark 2: Correct use of kinematic equation v² = u² + 2as

commonMistakes: Confusing degrees and radians - remember to convert using π/180
Thinking centripetal force acts outward instead of toward the center of the circle
Forgetting that objects in uniform circular motion are accelerating despite constant speed
Not recognizing that gravitational force follows an inverse square relationship with distance
Assuming gravitational field strength is constant everywhere rather than varying with distance in radial fields
examinerTips: Always check if angles are required in radians or degrees and convert appropriately
Remember that uniform spheres can be treated as point masses at their centers when calculating gravitational forces outside the sphere
formulas: For projectiles: ux = u cos θ, uy = u sin θ
note_data.keyDiagramDescription: Projectile motion diagram showing parabolic path with initial velocity components (ux horizontal, uy vertical), maximum height point, and range marked clearly with coordinate axes
pastPaperRefs: M/J 2023 P2 Q4 (6 marks) — they asked students to calculate displacement and velocity for a ball thrown vertically upward, including time to reach maximum height
O/N 2022 P1 Q8 (3 marks) — required interpretation of a velocity-time graph to find acceleration and distance traveled during different phases of motion
M/J 2022 P2 Q2 (5 marks) — tested understanding of equations of motion for a car braking uniformly, asking for stopping distance and time
O/N 2021 P2 Q6 (4 marks) — involved projectile motion calculation for horizontal and vertical components of velocity
M/J 2021 P1 Q12 (2 marks) — asked students to identify correct kinematic equation for given scenario involving constant acceleration

workedExample.question: A stone is thrown vertically upward with an initial velocity of 20 m/s. Calculate (a) the maximum height reached, (b) the time taken to return to the starting point. Take g = 10 m/s². (6 marks)
steps: Step 1: Identify given values: u = +20 m/s, a = g = -10 m/s² (negative because opposing motion), v = 0 m/s at maximum height
Step 2: For maximum height, use v² = u² + 2as. Substitute: 0² = 20² + 2(-10)s
Step 4: For total time, use s = ut + ½at². At return point, s = 0: 0 = 20t + ½(-10)t²
Step 5: Factor: 0 = t(20 - 5t), so t = 0 (start) or t = 4 s (return point)
workedExample.answer: (a) Maximum height = 20 m (b) Time to return = 4 s
workedExample.markScheme: 1 mark for correct equation selection, 1 mark for substitution, 2 marks for calculation of height, 2 marks for time calculation with correct reasoning

examinerWarnings: Examiner Report 2023: 'Many candidates confused velocity and speed, failing to include direction' — always specify direction when dealing with velocity in kinematics problems
Examiner Report 2022: 'Students frequently forgot to convert units before calculation' — always check that all quantities are in consistent SI units (m, s, m/s, m/s²)
Examiner Report 2021: 'Common error was using wrong sign for acceleration due to gravity' — remember g is negative when opposing motion direction, positive when supporting it
note_data.keyDiagram: Students must draw and label velocity-time graphs showing: (1) straight line for constant acceleration with gradient representing acceleration, (2) area under line representing displacement, (3) clearly marked axes with units (time in seconds, velocity in m/s), (4) ability to sketch curves for non-uniform acceleration. Include labeled points for initial velocity, final velocity, and time intervals.
note_data.quickRecap: Circular motion involves angular displacement, speed, and acceleration measured in radians. Objects in circular motion experience centripetal acceleration (v²/r) requiring centripetal force (mv²/r) toward the center. Gravitational fields create forces between masses following Newton's law F = Gm₁m₂/r². Field strength varies as g = GM/r² in radial fields but remains constant in uniform fields.
